sections.header: Verses 1-15 -- "The LORD Sent Me to Prophesy"

heb.paragraph: The priests and prophets demand: "You must die!" (mot tamut, v.8). The phrase is an emphatic infinitive absolute -- "dying you shall die," the same construction used in God's warning to Adam (Gen 2:17) and in capital punishment sentences. Jeremiah faces a formal death sentence for his Temple Sermon.

refs.note: Jesus's trial before the Sanhedrin follows the same pattern: religious authorities condemn the prophet for speaking truth about the temple.
Stephen's speech and martyrdom -- another prophet killed for challenging the temple establishment.

notes.note: The sermon summary: "If you do not listen... I will make this house like Shiloh." The threat to the temple's sanctity provokes the institutional response.
Priests, prophets, and people seize Jeremiah: "You must die!" The charge is essentially blasphemy -- threatening the temple is threatening God himself, in their theology.

Jeremiah's defense: "The LORD sent me." He does not plead for his life or modify his message. Instead, he warns his accusers: "If you put me to death, you will bring the guilt of innocent blood upon yourselves."
Calvin admires Jeremiah's courage: "The prophet does not beg for mercy or retract a syllable. He places his accusers on trial: their response to God's word will determine their own judgment."
The date "early in the reign of Jehoiakim" (609-608 BC) places this event shortly after Josiah's death at Megiddo -- the political transition that ended the reform era.
The Shiloh comparison was explosive: Shiloh's destruction (c. 1050 BC) was remembered as proof that God would abandon even his own dwelling place.
Lundbom identifies a standard trial narrative: charge (vv.7-9), defense (vv.12-15), verdict (vv.16-19). The form mirrors ancient Near Eastern legal proceedings.

Brueggemann reads the trial as "truth against power." The prophet has no institutional backing, no army, no allies -- only the claim "The LORD sent me." That claim is either the ultimate authority or the ultimate delusion.

hist.context: Chapter 26 provides the narrative account of the Temple Sermon (ch. 7) and its aftermath. Where chapter 7 records the sermon content, chapter 26 records the institutional response: arrest, trial, and near-execution. Jeremiah's defense is simple: "The LORD sent me" (v.12). He does not retract or soften the message but offers the people a choice: amend your ways and avoid the judgment, or kill the messenger and bring more guilt upon yourselves.

sections.header: Verses 16-24 -- Acquittal and the Precedent of Micah

heb.paragraph: The elders invoke the precedent of Micah the prophet (nabi', v.18), who prophesied Jerusalem's destruction under Hezekiah (Mic 3:12) and was not executed. The elders' argument is that punishing a prophet for delivering God's word would bring disaster, as happened when Jehoiakim killed the prophet Uriah (vv.20-23). The defense rests on prophetic precedent.

refs.note: The specific prophecy cited: "Zion will be plowed like a field, Jerusalem will become a heap of rubble." The same message Jeremiah delivers a century later.
Shaphan's family supported Josiah's reforms. Ahikam son of Shaphan now protects Jeremiah -- the reform party sustains the prophetic voice.

notes.note: The officials and elders overrule the priests: "This man should not be sentenced to death." The Micah precedent establishes that punishing a true prophet brings greater judgment, not safety.
The Uriah episode: Jehoiakim had the prophet Uriah extradited from Egypt and killed. This shows Jeremiah's vulnerability -- he survived where another prophet did not.
Ahikam son of Shaphan "supported Jeremiah, and so he was not handed over to the people to be put to death." Providence works through political allies.
Calvin commends the elders' reasoning: "Hezekiah feared the LORD and turned aside judgment. The right response to prophetic warning is repentance, not execution."
The citation of Micah 3:12 is the only instance in the Hebrew Bible where one prophet is quoted by name within another prophetic book -- a remarkable act of canonical cross-referencing.

Lundbom notes that the Uriah episode serves as a dark foil to Jeremiah's acquittal: two prophets, same message, different outcomes. Jeremiah's survival is contingent on political allies, not on the inherent protection of prophetic office.

Brueggemann identifies a "politics of prophecy" -- the survival of the prophetic voice depends on institutional allies. Ahikam's family protected both the scroll (Josiah's reform) and the prophet (Jeremiah's ministry). Truth needs political infrastructure to survive.

hist.context: The elders save Jeremiah by citing Micah's precedent: Hezekiah feared the LORD and sought his favor rather than executing the prophet. The counter-example of Uriah son of Shemaiah (vv.20-23) -- a prophet who delivered the same message as Jeremiah but was hunted down in Egypt and killed by Jehoiakim -- shows how close Jeremiah came to death. Ahikam son of Shaphan, from the influential Shaphan family that supported Josiah's reforms, protects Jeremiah.

rows.label: vv.12-15
rows.text: Jeremiah's Defense: The LORD Sent Me

lit.note: A courtroom drama with arrest, defense, precedent, and acquittal. The Uriah counter-example shows how close Jeremiah came to death.

themes.note: Prophecy at 9 -- the trial tests prophetic authority itself. Faith at 6: Jeremiah's trust that God sent him sustains his defense against the death sentence.

The elders' argument is that punishing a prophet for delivering God's word would bring disaster, as happened when Jehoiakim killed the prophet Uriah (vv.20-23). translations.text: Why do you prophesy in the Lord’s name that this house will be like Shiloh and this city will be desolate and deserted?” And all the people crowded around Jeremiah in the house of the Lord.

Why have you prophesied in the name of the LORD, saying, ‘This house shall be like Shiloh, and this city shall be desolate, without inhabitant’?” And all the people gathered around Jeremiah in the house of the LORD.
and if you do not listen to the words of my servants the prophets, whom I have sent to you again and again (though you have not listened),
and to listen to the words of my servants the prophets whom I send to you urgently, though you have not listened,

debate: The date "early in the reign of Jehoiakim" (609-608 BC) places this event shortly after Josiah's death at Megiddo -- the political transition that end…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Jeremiah's Trial within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Jesus's trial before the Sanhedrin follows the same pattern: religious authorities condemn the prophet for speaking truth about the temple.
Stephen's speech and martyrdom -- another prophet killed for challenging the temple establishment.
The specific prophecy cited: "Zion will be plowed like a field, Jerusalem will become a heap of rubble." The same message Jeremiah delivers a century later.
Shaphan's family supported Josiah's reforms. Ahikam son of Shaphan now protects Jeremiah -- the reform party sustains the prophetic voice.

coaching.tip: Jeremiah's temple sermon nearly gets him killed. His defense is simple: 'The LORD sent me to prophesy against this house' (v. 12). He doesn't soften the message to save himself. The prophet's authority comes from his sender, not his audience's approval. Jeremiah models what Jesus later articulates: 'Blessed are you when people persecute you' (Matthew 5:11).

The elders invoke Micah as precedent: when Micah prophesied Jerusalem's destruction, Hezekiah repented and disaster was averted (v. 18, quoting Micah 3:12). A century-old prophecy saves Jeremiah's life. This is the Old Testament citing itself as authority — canonical consciousness in action. Past prophecy becomes legal precedent: if Micah wasn't executed for similar words, neither should Jeremiah be.
